per_eval.delta: Both found every seeded defect. Skill adds: tier scorecard, stable rule ids, per-finding RFC/OWASP citations, a cannot-determine section, and correct Tier-1 ranking of the missing-exp bug (baseline ranked it medium). Skill correctly treated placeholder bcrypt hashes as fixture data rather than a finding.

per_eval.unintended_real_bug: password-change revokes only the presented cookie's token family; sessions from other logins survive (R12)
per_eval.delta: Both caught the unintended password-change bug. Neither flagged the OWASP denylist as a failure (lane discipline held with and without the skill). Skill graded C by rule (a confirmed Tier 2 failure caps at C) vs baseline's reviewer-judgment B+; the skill grade is more reproducible. One planned assertion (grade A/B) was voided because the fixture actually contains the bug.

Skill explicitly declared standards-depth for the non-JS stack and named the one skipped stack rule; baseline did not frame depth. Skill traced python-jose source to confirm the missing-exp default. Baseline found python-jose CVEs the skill's scope boundary excluded, the one respect in which the boundary may be too strict for the auth library itself.

conclusions: Zero confirmed false positives and zero fabricated file paths across all three with-skill runs, the primary failure mode for this genre.

The skill's value over a strong baseline is not raw detection (baseline found the seeded defects too) but structure: a reproducible tier-based grade, stable rule ids, standards citations, honest cross-stack depth labeling, and lane discipline that never faults a compliant OWASP alternative.
The skill costs ~1.7x the tokens and ~3x the wall-clock of an unstructured review, the price of reading four rule files and tracing evidence.

Two follow-ups surfaced: (1) consider allowing findings against the auth library itself (known CVEs) despite the general no-dependency-CVE boundary; (2) the solid fixture needs its password-change bug fixed so future iterations have a clean A/B control.
